pages.title: Mental Health Clinic in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Mental Health Clinic in Louisville, KY

Therapy, EMDR, family therapy, case management, and community support in Louisville with clear next steps for getting started.

Reach Out
Call (502) 416-1416

How To Start

Start with what is happening now. The office can help sort fit, availability, insurance, and the most practical next step.

1

Reach Out

Send the contact form or call with a brief description of what is happening.

2

Talk Through Your Needs

The office reviews fit, availability, insurance, and practical service options.

3

Complete Intake

You are routed toward the service or provider path that makes the most sense.

4

Schedule Support

Schedule with your provider in the office, by telehealth, or another approved setting.

Services For Individuals, Children, Couples, And Families

You do not have to diagnose the problem before reaching out. Use this page as one organized starting point for counseling, family support, court-related services, community-based services, group therapy, and trauma therapy.

Individual Counseling

One-on-one therapy for adults and adolescents who need steady support for anxiety, depression, trauma, stress, relationships, or major life changes.

Learn More

Child Therapy

Therapy for children and teens who need help with emotional regulation, behavior, trauma, family stress, school pressure, or developmental transitions.

Learn More

Family Therapy

Support for families working through conflict, transitions, attachment concerns, parenting stress, or communication patterns that need attention.

Learn More

Couples Counseling

Counseling for couples who need a structured place to work through communication, conflict, trust, or relationship stress.

Learn More

Court Ordered Services

Court-related services for families who need co-parenting support, reunification work, or structured documentation around participation.

Learn More

Community Based Services

Case management and community support for practical needs, skill building, coordination, and stability outside the therapy room.

Learn More

Group Therapy

Group support for people who benefit from structure, shared practice, and guidance with others working on related goals.

Learn More

Trauma Therapy

Trauma-focused therapy, including EMDR when appropriate, for people working through painful experiences, stress responses, or stuck patterns.

Learn More

Start With A Simple Request

Share a brief note about what is happening, who needs support, and whether you are looking for office-based, telehealth, home, school, or community-based services.

Reach Out

pages.title: Teen Therapy in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Child Therapy  /  Teen Therapy in Louisville, KY

Teen therapy gives teens a place to talk. It also helps parents make sense of changes at home. A family may reach out when a teen seems sad, worried, angry, shut down, or hard to reach.

Reach Out
Call (502) 416-1416

At Louisville Mental Health Group, teen counseling starts with what is happening now. We listen to the teen and the parent. Then we help the family find a plan that fits. The plan may change as needs become clear.

When This May Help

Teen therapy may help when a teen has:

  • Mood changes, sadness, or anger
  • Worry, panic, stress, or fear
  • School stress or trouble keeping up
  • Social stress or friendship problems
  • More time alone or less interest in things
  • Family conflict or trouble talking at home
  • Grief, trauma, or a major life change

Parents may see a change but not know why it began. Teens may also know that something feels wrong but have a hard time saying it. You do not need to know the cause before you reach out.

Some families are not sure where to start. Child Therapy is often for kids ages 2 to 12. Teen therapy is made for the needs of teens and their families.

What Support Can Look Like

Teen therapy often includes time for the teen to meet with the therapist alone. Parents or caregivers may also join check-ins or family sessions. At the first visit, the therapist explains what can stay private. The therapist also explains what may be shared and when a safety concern or law requires sharing. When possible, the therapist talks with the teen before sharing and shares only what is needed.

LMHG may use a team approach when it helps. A teen may have one main therapist. Another therapist may join if a special skill is needed. Each role should stay clear. For some older teens, Individual Counseling may be a better fit.

Teen Therapy, Child Therapy, And Family Work

Teen therapy gives teens more say in the work. It also gives them more room to talk alone. In child therapy, parents are often more involved. This is more common with young children.

Parents can still be part of teen therapy. They may share what has changed. They may help with home routines. They may also join a session when it makes sense.

At times, the main issue affects the whole home. In that case, Family Therapy may be added. It may also be the best place to start. The family and therapist can decide this together.

[lmhg_related_pages heading="Related Pages"] [lmhg_faqs heading="Common Questions"]

Ready To Reach Out?

Use the Reach Out intake form to ask about teen therapy. You can share contact details and the main concern. The form does not ask for clinical details. LMHG will follow up to talk about fit, timing, and the next step.

Reach Out

faqItems.question: Is teen therapy only for serious problems?
faqItems.answer: No. It can help with stress, worry, sadness, school pressure, social strain, family conflict, or a hard change.

faqItems.question: Will parents be part of teen therapy?
faqItems.answer: They may be. Parent check-ins or family sessions can be used when they fit the teen's needs.

faqItems.question: Can a teen have privacy in therapy?
faqItems.answer: Yes, but privacy has limits. At the first visit, the therapist explains what may be shared and when a safety concern or law requires sharing.

faqItems.question: Is teen therapy the same as family therapy?
faqItems.answer: No. Teen therapy centers on the teen. Family therapy works more on patterns and stress across the family.

Adults can ask about in-person counseling at the Louisville office or telehealth when it is clinically appropriate and available. The office can also talk through insurance, fit, and whether another LMHG service should be part of the plan. Counseling may help when you feel stretched thin, stuck, overwhelmed, disconnected, or ready to understand why the same pressure keeps building.

[lmhg_related_pages heading="Related Pages"] [lmhg_faqs heading="Common Questions"]

What To Expect In Anxiety And Depression Therapy

Start by naming the symptoms, routines, relationships, or responsibilities that feel hardest to carry. The office can help route the request to an appropriate therapy starting point.

Therapy can help identify patterns, triggers, symptoms, avoidance loops, and daily pressures while building a plan for steadier routines and emotional regulation. We support people across Louisville and nearby counties when work pressure, family stress, school demands, financial strain, or relationship conflict intensify anxiety and depression. This service may fit if racing thoughts, panic, low energy, hopelessness, irritability, numbness, or everyday responsibilities have started to feel bigger than your ability to keep up.

pages.title: Parent-Child Attachment Therapy in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Family Therapy  /  Parent-Child Attachment Therapy in Louisville, KY

At LMHG, parent-child attachment therapy helps a child and parent rebuild trust. It may fit after stress, time apart, loss, moves, or other hard change. The work looks at the bond between a child and parent.

Reach Out
Call (502) 416-1416

This work often fits within family therapy. It may also connect with child therapy when the child needs their own support.

When This May Help

You may be looking for help because:

  • A child pulls away or shuts down with a parent.
  • A child seems guarded or does not ask for comfort.
  • A parent is not sure how to respond to big feelings.
  • Trust has been hurt by stress, loss, or time apart.
  • A move or other family change has been hard.
  • Daily routines lead to fear or power struggles.

These signs do not tell us the cause on their own. A therapist can help the family look at the full picture and choose a clear plan.

What Support Can Look Like

Sessions may include the child, the parent, the family, or a mix. The plan will depend on the child's age, family history, safety needs, and current stress.

The therapist may help the parent spot patterns and slow down hard moments. Parents can learn ways to stay calm, set clear roles, and respond in ways the child can grasp. The child may get help naming feelings and making up after conflict.

The work may cover:

  • Calm home routines.
  • Repair after an argument.
  • Clear roles for adults and children.
  • Simple ways to talk about hard topics.
  • Parent coaching and check-ins.

Some families need help with one short-term strain. Others need a slower plan. LMHG does not assume one provider can meet every need. Another provider may help when a child or parent needs a different type of therapy.

How This Fits With Family And Child Therapy

Family therapy is broad. It may focus on conflict, routines, stress, or how family members talk with each other. Attachment therapy has a tighter focus on trust between a child and parent.

Child therapy gives a child their own place to work on feelings, behavior, stress, or trauma. When the main concern is the parent-child bond, the parent will often take part too.

LMHG must review court-related family needs before naming the right service. Attachment therapy is not a custody review, legal opinion, or promise that a family will reunite.

[lmhg_related_pages heading="Related Pages"] [lmhg_faqs heading="Common Questions"]

Ready To Reach Out?

Use the Reach Out intake form to ask about parent-child attachment therapy. Share a short note about the parent-child trust concern. The form does not ask for clinical details. Someone from LMHG will follow up. You do not need to pick the service on your own. We can help sort out the clearest first step.

Reach Out

faqItems.question: Is this page about adult attachment styles?
faqItems.answer: No. At LMHG, this service is only for trust and repair between a child and parent. LMHG is not adding a separate adult attachment page at this time.

faqItems.question: Does a parent take part?
faqItems.answer: Often, yes. Parent involvement matters when the main concern is the bond between the child and parent.

faqItems.question: Can this help after a family change?
faqItems.answer: It may fit when loss, time apart, a move, or another change has hurt trust. LMHG will review the details first.

faqItems.question: Is this the same as family therapy?
faqItems.answer: It may be part of family therapy, but it has a tighter focus on parent-child trust and repair.

pages.title: Mental Health Careers in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Mental Health Careers in Louisville, KY

Mental health careers in Louisville at LMHG for therapists, counselors, case managers, community support professionals, and care team roles that support accessible local care.

Work that connects clinical care with real Louisville needs.

Louisville Mental Health Group serves clients who may need office-based therapy, telehealth, child and family support, case management, community support, or coordinated care across home, school, and community settings. Careers here can involve direct clinical work, practical care coordination, documentation, scheduling support, and careful communication with clients and families.

If your background overlaps with individual counseling, child and teen therapy, family therapy, case management, community support services, or community-based mental health services, send a resume and a short note about the role you are interested in, your credentials or experience, and why the practice feels like a fit.

LMHG is especially interested in people who understand that Louisville mental health work is both clinical and practical: clients may need a provider who can listen well, explain options clearly, respect insurance and access barriers, and help families move from first contact to the right next step.

Career Fit Questions

What mental health roles can I ask LMHG about?

You can ask about therapist, counselor, case management, community support, administrative, and care-team roles. Current hiring needs may vary, so the best first step is to share your background and the role you are seeking.

What should I include when I reach out about a career?

Include a resume, your Kentucky credentials or relevant experience, the type of role you want, preferred setting, availability, and a short explanation of why LMHG's Louisville service mix fits your work.

Are LMHG roles only office-based?

Not necessarily. Depending on the role, service fit, and current needs, work may connect with office-based care, telehealth, home, school, or community settings. Ask about the setting expectations for the specific role.

Can newer clinicians or support professionals contact LMHG?

Yes. If your training, supervision needs, license status, or experience may fit LMHG's work, explain that clearly in your message so the office can respond based on current needs and requirements.

Ready To Talk About Fit?

Use the contact page for career questions, credentials, role fit, and practical next steps.

Contact LMHG
View Service Areas

pages.title: Child Behavioral Therapy in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Child Therapy  /  Child Behavioral Therapy in Louisville, KY

Child behavioral therapy helps parents make sense of behavior they see at home or school. The cause may not be clear yet. A therapist looks at what happens before, during, and after the behavior. Then the family can build a plan that fits the child.

Reach Out
Call (502) 416-1416

This service is often used with children ages 2 to 12. It is one type of child therapy, not a one-size plan for every child.

When This May Help

This service may help when a child has:

  • Big reactions that are hard to calm.
  • Hitting, yelling, running off, or frequent refusal.
  • School calls or trouble with class rules.
  • Hard mornings, bedtimes, or screen limits.
  • Meltdowns or shutdowns after a change.
  • Stress after grief, trauma, a move, or family conflict.
  • Behavior that leaves parents unsure what to try next.

Parents do not need to know why the behavior is happening before they reach out. Starting with what you see is enough.

What Support Can Look Like

The plan may include time with the child, parent check-ins, family sessions, or help with home routines. School contact may also be part of the plan when it is useful and allowed.

The child may learn to name feelings, calm their body, and recover after conflict. Parents may learn how to use clear limits and steady follow-through. Younger children often need more parent help. Older children may spend more time alone with the therapist.

LMHG does not use the same plan for every child. Some children need more play-based work. Some need more work on routines and skills. Family therapy may help when stress involves more than one person at home.

This service does not include psychiatry or medicine. LMHG does not provide autism or learning tests. We also do not provide ABA, speech therapy, or occupational therapy. This service does not include custody opinions or crisis response.

How This Differs From Child Therapy And Play Therapy

Child therapy is the broad parent service. It can help with feelings, grief, trauma, school stress, behavior, or family change.

Child behavioral therapy has a closer focus on behavior that affects daily life. It looks at patterns, triggers, routines, skills, and parent response.

Play therapy uses play and other tools that fit the child's age. It can help a child show feelings that are hard to put into words. Child behavioral work may use play too, but its main focus stays on behavior patterns and daily skills.

[lmhg_related_pages heading="Related Pages"] [lmhg_faqs heading="Common Questions"]

Ready To Reach Out?

Use the Reach Out intake form to share a short note about what you see. The form does not ask for clinical details. Someone from LMHG will follow up about fit, time, and next steps. Clients can often be seen within 7 to 14 days. The wait may be longer when a family needs a certain provider or schedule.

Reach Out

faqItems.question: Does this service include medicine?
faqItems.answer: No. LMHG does not provide psychiatry or medicine management.

faqItems.question: Does LMHG provide autism or learning tests?
faqItems.answer: No. LMHG does not provide autism tests, learning tests, ABA, speech therapy, or occupational therapy.

faqItems.question: Will the therapist give a custody opinion?
faqItems.answer: No. This service does not include custody opinions, custody reviews, or legal advice.

faqItems.question: Is this crisis support?
faqItems.answer: No. This is not a crisis response service. Use emergency or crisis resources when there is an immediate safety concern.

pages.title: Conflict Resolution Counseling in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Family Therapy  /  Conflict Resolution Counseling in Louisville, KY

Hard talks can get stuck. They may turn into fights, blame, or long silence. The goal is not to decide who is right. The goal is to slow the pattern. It is also to see what is going on. This can give hard talks more structure.

Reach Out
Call (502) 416-1416

This work is for conflict among family members who are not a couple. It may include parents and adult children, siblings, parents and grandparents, or members of a blended family. Who takes part will depend on the conflict and the plan.

When This May Help

This work may help when:

  • the same fight keeps coming back.
  • one person gets louder while another shuts down.
  • family members talk through other people. They do not talk to each other.
  • parenting rules cause conflict across homes. They may also cause conflict across generations.
  • siblings or blended family members feel stuck in old roles.
  • hurt feelings make it hard to listen or repair.
  • a hard choice needs a calm and useful talk.

You do not have to wait until each talk becomes a fight. Some families seek help when they first see a pattern. They want to change it before it grows.

What Support Can Look Like

A therapist may help each person see the fight as a whole. This includes what happens before, during, and after it. Sessions can help people slow down and name the real issue. People can learn to listen before they plan a reply. They can also ask for change in a clear way.

People may practice taking a break without dropping the issue. They may learn to return to the talk and set a clear limit. They may also learn to repair harm after someone gets hurt.

The therapist does not pick a winner or take sides. Each person looks at what they do. LMHG cannot promise that things will change.

LMHG may use more than one provider when roles are clear. One person may also have their own therapist. A different therapist may lead this work.

Conflict Resolution, Family Therapy, And Couples Counseling

Family Therapy is broad. It may look at family habits, stress, and daily life. It may also look at roles and how people talk. This page has a tighter aim. It works on a fight that repeats or keeps the family stuck.

Couples Counseling is for two romantic partners. It may cover trust, closeness, sex, money, parenting stress, and big choices. Conflict Resolution Counseling is for non-couple family relationships.

This is therapy. It is not a legal service or custody review. It does not settle a case or make a binding deal. LMHG does not give legal advice or promise a court result.

[lmhg_related_pages heading="Related Pages"] [lmhg_faqs heading="Common Questions"]

Ready To Reach Out?

Use the Reach Out form to share your contact details. Tell us who is involved and add a short note about the conflict. You do not need to know whether Family Therapy or conflict-focused work fits best. Someone from LMHG will follow up about fit and next steps.

Reach Out

faqItems.question: Is this for couples?
faqItems.answer: No. Couples Counseling is for romantic partners. This service is for conflict among other family members, such as parents and adult children, siblings, parents and grandparents, or members of a blended family.

faqItems.question: Is this the same as mediation?
faqItems.answer: No. This is therapy. It works on habits, how people talk, and how they make up after a fight. LMHG does not settle legal cases or make binding deals.

faqItems.question: Does everyone have to attend every session?
faqItems.answer: Not always. The therapist will help decide who should come. The plan will fit the goals and each provider's role.

faqItems.question: Will the therapist decide who is right?
faqItems.answer: No. The therapist helps people see the pattern and have a more useful talk. The therapist does not take sides.

pages.title: Couples Counseling in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Couples Counseling in Louisville, KY

Couples counseling gives two partners a structured place to talk through hard issues with a therapist. LMHG works with same-sex and opposite-sex couples, whether they are married or not. You do not have to agree on the cause of the problem before you start.

Reach Out
Call (502) 416-1416

When This May Help

Couples therapy may help with:

  • Talks that turn into the same argument.
  • A loss of trust.
  • Feeling far apart or alone in the relationship.
  • Parenting stress.
  • Sex or intimacy concerns.
  • Money stress.
  • A major choice or life change.
  • Thoughts about separation.
  • Doubt about whether to stay together.

You can come in when a problem is new or when it has been present for years. Therapy does not promise that the relationship will stay the same or be repaired. It gives both partners a clear way to talk and look at next steps.

What Support Can Look Like

The therapist helps set a plan for the talks. This may include slowing the pace, naming the main issue, and making sure each partner has time to speak. The work can help you hear what the other person means, even when you do not agree.

Both people have a say. They help set the plan. The plan can change as the work moves forward. The pace can slow when talks get tense. The work may start with one hard event. It may also look at a pattern that keeps coming back. You can plan how to talk at home. The topic may be parenting, money, sex, trust, or a major choice.

The therapist does not take one partner's side. The role is to keep the talk fair, clear, and tied to the goals both partners set.

Couples, Relationship, And Marriage Counseling

Couples counseling, relationship counseling, and marriage counseling often mean the same kind of service. At LMHG, Couples Counseling is the main name. It is for two romantic partners who want help with trust, closeness, patterns that repeat, or hard choices about the relationship. Marriage is not required.

Conflict Resolution Counseling has a different focus. It helps with conflict among non-couple family members, such as parents and adult children, siblings, grandparents, or blended family members. Couples Counseling stays focused on the two partners and the goals they bring.

One or both partners may also use Individual Counseling. When that happens, a different therapist should lead the couple's work. Separate therapists keep the roles clear and give each service its own focus.

[lmhg_related_pages heading="Related Pages"] [lmhg_faqs heading="Common Questions"]

Ready To Reach Out?

Use the Reach Out intake form to share your contact details and a short note about the support you want. The form does not ask for clinical details. LMHG will follow up about fit, timing, payment, and next steps.

Reach Out

faqItems.question: Do we have to be married?
faqItems.answer: No. LMHG works with married and unmarried couples, including same-sex and opposite-sex couples.

faqItems.question: Does LMHG offer marriage counseling?
faqItems.answer: Yes. Marriage counseling is available as couples counseling for married partners.

faqItems.question: Is relationship counseling the same as couples counseling?
faqItems.answer: The terms often overlap. LMHG uses Couples Counseling as the main name for work with married or unmarried romantic partners.

faqItems.question: Can one or both partners have individual therapy too?
faqItems.answer: Yes. A separate therapist should provide individual therapy so each clinician has a clear role.

faqItems.question: Will the therapist tell us who is right?
faqItems.answer: No. The therapist does not take sides or give legal or custody opinions.

pages.title: Mental Health Providers in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Team

Meet the Louisville Mental Health Group team serving clients across Louisville with therapy, family support, case management, and community-based mental health care.

Reach Out
View Services

A Louisville care team built around fit, access, and follow-through.

Finding a mental health provider in Louisville often starts with a practical question: who is the right fit for the concern, schedule, insurance, and level of support needed right now? LMHG helps clients sort those details before care begins, whether the next step is individual counseling, child or teen therapy, family therapy, case management, or community-based services.

The team supports people across Louisville and nearby Kentucky communities who are dealing with anxiety, depression, trauma, family stress, behavior concerns, relationship strain, school challenges, and day-to-day stability needs. If you are unsure where to begin, the office can help route your question toward the service path that fits best.

For questions about coverage, visit insurance and Medicaid information. For scheduling, provider fit, or general next steps, use the contact page or the intake link below.

Provider Fit Questions

How do I choose the right LMHG provider?

Start with the main concern, age of the client, preferred setting, insurance, and scheduling needs. The office can help clarify whether therapy, family support, case management, community support, or another service is the better first step.

Can I ask about provider fit before scheduling?

Yes. Share what is happening, who needs support, and what has or has not helped before. That information helps the team discuss fit, availability, and the most practical next step.

Does the team work with children, teens, adults, couples, and families?

LMHG has service pages for children and teens, adults, couples, families, trauma, group therapy, and community-based care. The exact provider and service fit depends on the concern, availability, and clinical appropriateness.

What if my needs go beyond weekly therapy?

Some clients also need help with coordination, resources, routines, or community stability. In those cases, the office may discuss case management, community support, in-home support, or school/community-based options when appropriate.

Need Help Contacting The Office?

Reach out with provider-fit, availability, insurance, scheduling, or contact questions.

pages.title: Parenting Support in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Services  /  Child Therapy  /  Parenting Support in Louisville, KY

Parenting support at Louisville Mental Health Group helps parents work through hard moments at home. It gives them a place to slow down, spot patterns, and choose clear next steps. The focus may be behavior, big feelings, limits, routines, school stress, or family strain.

Reach Out
Call (502) 416-1416

This service is for parents. It is part of child therapy and gives parents focused help with what is happening at home. It is not a parenting class, a program that gives a certificate, a custody review, or a legal service.

When This May Help

Parenting support may fit when:

  • Daily routines turn into fights or shutdowns.
  • A child has big reactions that are hard to calm.
  • Parents do not agree on rules or limits.
  • School stress is making home life harder.
  • Bedtime, mornings, screens, or changes feel stuck.
  • A loss, move, divorce, or other stress has changed home life.
  • A parent feels worn down and is not sure what to try.

You do not need to know the root cause before reaching out. Start with what you can see and what feels hard now.

What Support Can Look Like

Parenting support is parent-only work. It may include parent sessions, check-ins, and plans for home routines. A therapist may help parents notice what happens before conflict. Parents can practice clear limits, calm replies, and steady follow-through.

The work may also cover how to repair after an argument. School or family contact may be part of the plan when it is useful and allowed.

LMHG does not assume one plan fits every home. Some parents need a few visits for one concern. Some children need their own therapist or family sessions through another part of child therapy. More than one provider may help when each person has a clear need.

How Parenting Support Is Different

Parenting support is not a class. It does not follow one set lesson plan or promise a certificate. It is parent-only counseling and guidance based on what is happening in your home.

Family therapy looks at the wider family pattern. It may include parents, children, siblings, grandparents, or others. Parenting support keeps the main focus on the adult who cares for the child.

Child therapy is the parent service that includes parenting support. A child may have their own therapy while a parent also gets help with rules, routines, and home stress.

For separated parents who need help working with each other, co-parenting services may be a better fit.

[lmhg_related_pages heading="Related Pages"] [lmhg_faqs heading="Common Questions"]

Ready To Reach Out?

Use the Reach Out intake form to ask about parenting support. Share a short note about what feels hardest. The form does not ask for clinical details. Someone from LMHG will follow up about fit, time, payment, and the next step.

You do not need to know which service to pick. We can help sort that out with you.

Reach Out

faqItems.answer: No. Parenting support is for parents. A child may take part in another part of child therapy or family therapy when that fits the plan.

faqItems.question: Can this connect with child therapy?
faqItems.answer: Yes. A child may have their own therapist while a parent gets help with home life.

faqItems.question: Does this include custody advice?
faqItems.answer: No. Parenting support does not include legal advice, custody opinions, custody reviews, or court outcome promises.

pages.title: Family Therapy vs. Individual Therapy | Which Fits?
pages.content:
Home  /  Articles  /  Family Therapy vs. Individual Therapy | Which Fits?

Family vs. individual therapy is not a competition between two choices. They answer different questions. Individual counseling focuses on one person's symptoms, stress, history, coping skills, and goals. Family therapy focuses on the patterns between people, including communication, conflict, parenting stress, trust, and shared routines.

The best starting point depends on where the problem is showing up most clearly. Some people need one private therapy space first. Some families need to work on the home pattern together. In some situations, both approaches can support the same larger goal.

Start With Individual Therapy When The Concern Is Mostly Internal

Individual therapy may be the better starting point when the main concern is anxiety, depression, trauma, grief, stress, mood changes, or a personal transition that feels hard to carry. It can also help when you need a private place to understand your own reactions before involving other people.

Start With Family Therapy When The Pattern Is Between People

Family therapy may be a better fit when the concern lives inside the family system. That can include repeated arguments, emotional distance, parenting strain, child behavior concerns, reunification stress, caregiver burnout, or communication that breaks down quickly.

Child Or Teen Concerns May Need Both Views

When a child or teen is struggling, the right path may include both individual support and family involvement. A young person may need space to talk, and caregivers may need help understanding behavior, emotional regulation, school pressure, or family stress.

A Practical Way To Decide

Ask where the main difficulty seems to live right now: inside one person, between people, or both at the same time. A provider can help you sort through that question and decide whether individual therapy, family therapy, or a combination makes the most sense.

The Next Step

You do not need to choose perfectly before reaching out. If the concern involves both personal symptoms and family stress, explain both. Louisville Mental Health Group can help compare service paths before care begins.

Ready To Reach Out?

Use the intake form to ask about fit, availability, insurance, and the best next step for starting care.

Reach Out

pages.title: Guide to Individual Therapy | What to Expect
pages.content:
Home  /  Articles  /  Guide to Individual Therapy | What to Expect

Individual therapy is a one-on-one service for people who want help with anxiety, depression, trauma, grief, stress, relationship patterns, life transitions, or emotional overwhelm. It gives you a private place to understand what is happening and practice a more workable response.

You do not need to arrive with perfect language for the problem. Many people begin with a simple sentence: "I am not managing this the way I want to."

What Individual Therapy Is

Individual therapy is a confidential process with a licensed mental health provider. Sessions focus on your symptoms, experiences, goals, relationships, coping patterns, and the pressures that are most active in your life.

What Usually Happens In Sessions

The first few sessions usually focus on understanding what brings you to therapy, what has already been tried, and what would make care feel useful. Sessions often last about 45 to 60 minutes, with frequency depending on clinical need, availability, and provider recommendation.

  • Check in on recent stress, symptoms, and progress.
  • Explore thoughts, emotions, behaviors, and relationship patterns.
  • Learn coping tools or communication strategies.
  • Identify goals that are specific enough to guide the work.
  • Talk about what is helping and what needs adjustment.

When Individual Therapy May Help

Individual therapy may be worth considering when anxiety, depression, stress, grief, trauma, relationship strain, or life transitions begin affecting how you sleep, work, parent, communicate, or care for yourself.

How To Choose A Good Fit

Provider fit matters. Consider what concerns you want help with, whether you prefer in-person care or telehealth, what insurance questions need to be answered, and whether you want a provider with experience in the concern you are bringing.

How To Get Started

You can start by reviewing the individual counseling page or contacting the office with the main concern, insurance information, and scheduling needs. If another service is a better fit, the office can help route the request.

Ready To Reach Out?

Use the intake form to ask about fit, availability, insurance, and the best next step for starting care.

Reach Out

pages.title: How to Talk to Someone About Therapy
pages.content:
Home  /  Articles  /  How to Talk to Someone About Therapy

Talking about therapy can be part of starting care, but telling people close to you may still feel uncomfortable. The goal is not to convince everyone immediately. The goal is to communicate clearly, protect your decision, and invite support where it is healthy to do so.

Know Why You Are Starting

Before the conversation, get clear on your own reason. You might be seeking help for anxiety, depression, grief, relationship stress, family conflict, trauma, parenting pressure, or simply feeling stuck.

Choose A Calmer Moment

Avoid bringing it up during an argument, right before work, or when everyone is distracted. Choose a private moment when there is enough time to speak without rushing.

Use Direct, Non-Blaming Language

You can explain your decision without making someone else responsible for it. Keep the conversation grounded in what you are working on and what kind of support you are choosing.

Be Ready For Questions

Loved ones may ask whether you are okay, whether therapy means something serious happened, or whether they did something wrong. You can answer briefly and repeat the main point.

Set Boundaries If The Response Is Not Helpful

Some people minimize therapy, ask intrusive questions, or respond with guilt. You can stay respectful without giving up your boundary.

Invite Support Without Pressure

If the person is safe and supportive, tell them what would help. That might be encouragement, privacy, transportation help, childcare, or simply not turning therapy into a debate.

A Practical Next Step

If you are ready to start care in the Louisville area, Louisville Mental Health Group can help compare individual counseling, family therapy, couples counseling, and other support options.

Ready To Reach Out?

Use the intake form to ask about fit, availability, insurance, and the best next step for starting care.

Reach Out

pages.title: When to Seek Therapy | 5 Signs Support May Help
pages.content:
Home  /  Articles  /  When to Seek Therapy | 5 Signs Support May Help

Therapy may be worth considering when emotional strain starts affecting your sleep, work, school, parenting, relationships, routines, or ability to feel like yourself. You do not have to wait for a crisis.

1. Anxiety, Sadness, Or Irritability Is Staying Around

Persistent anxiety, depression, numbness, panic, irritability, or sadness can be a sign that extra support is needed.

2. Relationships Keep Getting Pulled Into The Same Pattern

Therapy can help when conflict, distance, resentment, mistrust, or communication problems keep repeating.

3. Coping Strategies Are Creating More Problems

If coping strategies are starting to create new problems, therapy may help. That can include substance use, overeating or restriction, isolation, compulsive spending, self-harm, excessive screen use, or avoiding responsibilities because they feel too overwhelming.

4. Daily Life Feels Harder To Maintain

Mental health concerns can affect basic functioning, including sleep, hygiene, work, school, parenting, communication, and ordinary routines.

5. A Major Change Or Trauma Is Still Affecting You

Divorce, grief, job loss, medical stress, relocation, family conflict, violence, or other traumatic experiences can leave the nervous system and relationships unsettled long after the event itself.

Taking The Next Step

If several signs feel familiar, reaching out can help clarify the right service instead of guessing alone. Start with what is happening now, and the next step can become clearer.

Ready To Reach Out?

Use the intake form to ask about fit, availability, insurance, and the best next step for starting care.

Reach Out

pages.title: What to Expect When Starting Therapy
pages.content:
Home  /  Articles  /  What to Expect When Starting Therapy

Starting therapy does not require having everything figured out before you call. Many people begin with a general sense that something needs to change, even if they are not sure which service, clinician, or care setting is the best fit.

The first step is usually a conversation about what is happening now, what kind of support may fit, and whether care should happen at the Louisville office, by telehealth, or in another appropriate setting.

What The First Contact Usually Covers

The office may ask about the main concern, the age of the client, insurance, scheduling needs, location, and whether there are safety, court, school, or family factors that should be understood early.

What Happens After That

The goal is to identify a next step that is clear and manageable. That may be individual counseling, child counseling, family therapy, couples counseling, case management, community support, EMDR, or another service path.

If the first option is not the best fit, the conversation can still help narrow the direction.

Ready To Reach Out?

Use the intake form to ask about fit, availability, insurance, and the best next step for starting care.

Reach Out

pages.title: About Louisville Mental Health Group
pages.content:
Home  /  FAQ  /  About LMHG

Learn about Louisville Mental Health Group, a local practice offering therapy, counseling, case management, court-related family support, and community-based services.

Reach Out
Meet The Team

LMHG helps people find a practical starting point.

You do not need to know the exact service before reaching out. Start with the main concern, age of the client, insurance questions, and preferred care setting.

What does Louisville Mental Health Group provide?

LMHG provides therapy, counseling, community-based support, court-related family services, group therapy, and specialty options such as EMDR from one Louisville-based practice.

Do I need to know the exact service before reaching out?

No. You can start with the main concern, age of the client, insurance questions, and preferred care setting. The office can help route the request.

Does LMHG have multiple clinic locations?

No. LMHG has one physical Louisville office. Depending on fit and availability, care may also involve telehealth, in-home support, school coordination, or community-based services.

Ready To Reach Out?

Use the intake form or call the office with the main concern, insurance questions, preferred care setting, and any timing needs.

Reach Out

pages.title: Therapy Cost in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  FAQ  /  Therapy Cost in Louisville, KY

Answers about therapy cost in Louisville, Medicaid, commercial insurance, private pay, coverage checks, and cost questions for LMHG mental health services.

Reach Out
Insurance Details

Cost questions need situation-specific confirmation.

Coverage, eligibility, provider fit, service availability, deductibles, copays, authorizations, and private-pay details may vary by plan and service.

Does LMHG accept insurance?

LMHG commonly works with Medicaid plans, many commercial insurance plans, and private pay, but coverage should be verified before care begins.

Can I ask about cost before scheduling?

Yes. Use intake or call the office with insurance details and the service you are considering so the team can help clarify next steps.

What information helps with a coverage question?

Have the insurance plan name, member information, the requested service, and any practical constraints ready when you contact the office.

Is a general insurance list a guarantee of coverage?

No. General coverage language is not a guarantee. Eligibility, benefits, provider fit, service availability, and authorization requirements still need to be confirmed for your situation.

Ready To Ask About Coverage?

Use the intake form or call the office with insurance details and the service you are considering so the team can help clarify next steps.

Reach Out

pages.content:

The Louisville Mental Health Group approach helps clients find a practical starting point for therapy, case management, community support, and care settings.

Reach Out
Browse Services

Fit comes from the concern, the setting, and the next practical step.

The team considers the concern, family context, age, setting, insurance, and availability before helping identify a realistic starting point.

How does LMHG help people choose a starting point?

The team considers the concern, family context, client age, care setting, insurance, and availability before helping identify the most practical next step.

Does care only happen in the office?

No. Depending on fit and availability, care may involve the Louisville office, telehealth, in-home support, school coordination, or community-based services.

What if someone needs more than one type of support?

Some clients and families need a combination of therapy, care coordination, family support, community support, or referrals. Intake helps identify the best place to begin.

Ready To Find The Next Step?

Use the intake form or call the office with the main concern and any practical constraints. The team can help route the request from there.

pages.title: Community-Based Mental Health Care
pages.content:
Home  /  Locations  /  Community-Based Mental Health Care in Louisville, KY

Community-based mental health care in Louisville can connect support with resources, care coordination, daily-life barriers, skill-building, and follow-through outside the office.

Reach Out
Community Services

Community care connects support to real-world context.

Some services work best when treatment goals connect with the systems clients already navigate, such as resources, appointments, family logistics, benefits, and daily routines.

Common Questions

What is community-based mental health care?

Community-based care connects support with real-world needs such as resources, appointments, routines, care coordination, and follow-through outside a traditional office visit.

Is community-based care the same as therapy?

Not always. Depending on the situation, it may involve case management, community support, skills work, coordination, or therapy-connected support.

How is the setting decided?

Setting depends on the concern, safety needs, service fit, location, insurance, provider availability, and what is practical for the client or family.

Start With A Simple Request

Share a brief note about what is happening, who needs support, and whether you are looking for office-based, telehealth, home, school, or community-based services.

Reach Out

pages.title: In-Home Therapy in Louisville, KY
pages.content:
Home  /  Locations  /  In-Home Therapy in Louisville, KY

In-home mental health therapy is a therapy visit held in the client's home in Louisville or a surrounding area. It is not home health care or physical therapy. It is also not a separate type of therapy. The therapist still picks an approach. It is based on the client's needs and goals. The home is simply where the visit takes place.

Reach Out

For some clients and families, meeting at home can help. The therapist can learn about home routines and stress. The family can talk about needs as they come up in daily life. In-home therapy is offered only when the setting is a good fit.

When This May Help

In-home therapy may be worth asking about when:

  • travel makes office visits hard.
  • home routines are a main part of the concern.
  • family stress is easier to understand at home.
  • a child has needs that show up most in the home.
  • the therapist needs to see how a daily pattern works.
  • office-only care does not show the full picture.

An in-home visit is not right for every person or concern. Fit depends on the service and the client's needs. Safety, location, insurance, and an open provider also matter.

What Support Can Look Like

An in-home session is a planned therapy visit. It is still mental health care. The therapist may meet with one person. Family may join when that is part of the plan. A session may focus on feelings, behavior, talking, coping skills, or home routines. The therapist and client choose what makes sense.

The plan should fit the client. Some people may use home visits for part of their care. They may use office or online visits at other times. Others may find that family therapy or another service fits better.

Commercial insurance or Medicaid may cover in-home therapy. Coverage is not promised. LMHG must check the plan, cost, service fit, and open providers. The costs and insurance page gives general payment details.

In-Home Therapy and Community Support Are Different

In-home therapy is therapy from a therapist in the home. Community Support is a separate service. An associate helps a client practice therapy goals. This may happen in the community. The associate also works with the client's regular therapist.

The main difference is the job, not just the place. A therapist gives therapy. A Community Support Associate helps with practice outside therapy. LMHG may use more than one provider when that fits the plan.

Common Questions

Is in-home therapy the same as home health or physical therapy?

No. LMHG provides mental health therapy in the home. It does not provide home health care or physical therapy.

Is in-home therapy available to everyone?

No. Need, fit, safety, location, insurance, and open provider times all matter.

Does insurance cover in-home therapy?

Commercial insurance or Medicaid may cover it. LMHG must check the plan and service before care starts.

Can family members join an in-home session?

They may join when the therapist and client agree that family input fits the therapy plan.

Ready To Reach Out?

Use the Reach Out intake form. Tell us who needs help and why home visits may work better. You do not need to share private health details on the form. Our team will check fit, safety, location, insurance, and open providers. Asking about in-home therapy does not mean home visits will be open.

Reach Out

faqItems.answer: No. Need, fit, safety, location, insurance, and open provider times all matter.

faqItems.question: Does insurance cover in-home therapy?
faqItems.answer: Commercial insurance or Medicaid may cover it. LMHG must check the plan and service before care starts.

faqItems.answer: They may join when the therapist and client agree that family input fits the therapy plan.

The Bardstown Road office is the physical clinic location.

For many clients, office visits create clearer structure, fewer distractions, and a dedicated setting for therapy or support.

Common Questions

Where is the Louisville office?

The office is at 4229 Bardstown Rd, Suite 310, Louisville, KY 40218. Contact the office before visiting so fit, availability, insurance, and next steps can be confirmed.

When does in-person counseling fit best?

Office-based care may fit clients who want a private setting, a predictable appointment routine, fewer home distractions, or face-to-face support from a provider.

Can I ask about telehealth or another setting instead?

Yes. The first contact can include questions about office visits, telehealth, community-based care, in-home support, or school coordination when those settings may be relevant.

Online care may be useful for working adults, busy caregivers, college students, or clients outside the immediate Louisville core when it is clinically appropriate and available.

Who can use online therapy in Kentucky?

Telehealth may be available for Kentucky clients when online care is clinically appropriate, legally permitted, and available for the service and provider involved.

Is every LMHG service available online?

pages.title: School-Based Mental Health Support
pages.content:
Home  /  Locations  /  School-Based Mental Health Support in Louisville, KY

School-based mental health support in Louisville may help when child and family concerns are tied to behavior, stress, routines, caregivers, and coordination.

Reach Out
Child Counseling

School coordination depends on fit, consent, and availability.

For some families, care is most effective when services can be coordinated with school demands, behavior concerns, emotional regulation, attendance, and educational routines.

Common Questions

Does LMHG provide services at every school?

No. School-based support depends on school coordination, consent, location, provider capacity, insurance, and whether the service is appropriate for the concern.

When can school-based support help?

School-based support may help when behavior, emotional regulation, attendance, family-school communication, or school routines are central to the child or teen concern.

What is needed before school coordination?

The office can help clarify whether consent, releases, school contact, provider availability, and service fit are in place before any school-based support is considered.
